summary:
The Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer, adopted on September 16, 1987, has been ratified by every nation on Earth — making it the only universally ratified treaty in UN history — and is widely regarded as the most successful international environmental agreement ever concluded. The protocol established binding schedules for the phase-out of chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), halons, and other ozone-depleting substances following the [[event:3683|discovery of the ozone hole]] over Antarctica.

The treaty was strengthened through successive amendments — London (1990), Copenhagen (1992), Montreal (1997), and Beijing (1999) — that accelerated phase-out schedules and added additional controlled substances. Developed countries phased out CFC production by 1996; developing countries received additional time under the principle of common but differentiated responsibilities. The protocol's effectiveness has been demonstrated by the measurable recovery of the ozone layer: the Antarctic ozone hole began to stabilize and showed early signs of healing by the early twenty-first century, though full recovery is not expected until the mid-twenty-first century. Former UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan called it perhaps the single most successful international agreement to date.
